questions.question: Which wireless charging standard uses magnetic induction?
questions.explanation: Qi (pronounced 'chee') is the most common wireless charging standard, using magnetic induction to transfer power.

questions.question: What does GPS stand for?
questions.explanation: GPS (Global Positioning System) uses satellites to determine precise geographic location.

questions.question: Which display technology uses organic compounds that emit light when an electric current passes through?
questions.explanation: OLED (Organic Light-Emitting Diode) uses organic compounds that emit light when current flows, providing better contrast than LCD.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a digitizer in a touchscreen device?
options: A. Display images on the screen
B. Convert touch input into digital signals
questions.explanation: The digitizer is a layer over the display that detects touch input and converts it into digital signals the device can process.

questions.question: Which mobile device feature uses accelerometers and gyroscopes?
questions.explanation: Accelerometers detect orientation/tilt, gyroscopes detect rotation. Together they enable auto-rotate, gaming motion controls, and step counting.

questions.question: What is the maximum length of a Cat5e Ethernet cable run?
questions.explanation: The maximum recommended length for Cat5e/Cat6 Ethernet cable is 100 meters (328 feet) per the IEEE 802.3 standard.

questions.question: Which connector type is used for fiber optic cables?
questions.explanation: LC (Lucent Connector) and SC (Subscriber Connector) are standard fiber optic connectors. RJ-45 is for copper Ethernet.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a MAC address?
options: A. Identify a device on the internet
B. Uniquely identify a network interface card
C. Assign an IP address automatically
questions.explanation: MAC (Media Access Control) address is a unique hardware identifier burned into every network interface card (NIC).

questions.question: Which wireless standard provides the fastest speed?
questions.explanation: 802.11ax (WiFi 6) offers up to 9.6 Gbps theoretical speed, faster than 802.11ac (WiFi 5) at 3.5 Gbps.

questions.question: What does DHCP dynamically assign to devices?
questions.explanation: DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) automatically assigns IP addresses, subnet masks, gateways, and DNS to devices on a network.

questions.question: What does RAM stand for?
questions.explanation: RAM (Random Access Memory) is volatile memory used for temporary data storage while the computer is running.

questions.question: Which CPU component performs arithmetic and logical operations?
questions.explanation: The ALU performs arithmetic (add, subtract) and logical (AND, OR, NOT) operations. The Control Unit manages instruction execution.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a UPS?
options: B. Provide backup power during outages
questions.explanation: UPS (Uninterruptible Power Supply) provides battery backup power during electrical outages, allowing graceful shutdown.

questions.question: Which storage type has no moving parts and uses flash memory?
questions.explanation: SSD (Solid State Drive) uses flash memory with no moving parts, providing faster access and better durability than HDDs.

questions.question: What does the PSU do in a computer?
options: B. Convert AC power to DC power for components
questions.explanation: PSU (Power Supply Unit) converts AC wall power to various DC voltages (+12V, +5V, +3.3V) needed by computer components.

questions.question: Which port is commonly used for modern video output on graphics cards?
questions.explanation: Modern graphics cards typically include HDMI, DisplayPort, DVI, and sometimes VGA ports for video output.

questions.question: What is the function of a heatsink?
options: B. Dissipate heat from the CPU/GPU
questions.explanation: A heatsink draws heat away from hot components (CPU, GPU) using metal fins that radiate heat into the air.

questions.question: What does the POST sequence do?
options: B. Test hardware components before boot
questions.explanation: POST (Power-On Self Test) checks critical hardware (RAM, CPU, GPU) before the system boots. Beeps indicate errors.

questions.question: What is a virtual machine?
options: B. A software emulation of a computer system
questions.explanation: A VM is a software-based emulation of a physical computer, running an OS and applications like a real machine.

questions.question: Which cloud model provides services over the internet to the general public?
questions.explanation: Public cloud (AWS, Azure, GCP) provides services to anyone over the internet. Private cloud is for single organization use.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of IaaS?
options: A. Pre-built applications ready to use
B. Virtualized computing resources over the internet
questions.explanation: IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) provides virtualized computing resources (servers, storage, networking) over the internet.

questions.question: Which Windows command opens the System Configuration utility?
questions.explanation: msconfig (System Configuration) manages startup programs, boot options, and services. regedit is for the registry.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Windows Task Manager?
options: B. View and manage running processes and performance
questions.explanation: Task Manager shows running processes, CPU/memory usage, startup programs, and allows terminating unresponsive applications.

questions.question: Which Linux command displays disk usage of directories?
questions.explanation: du -sh (disk usage, summarize, human-readable) shows the size of directories. df shows filesystem free space.

questions.question: What is the purpose of multifactor authentication?
options: B. Add extra layers of security beyond passwords
questions.explanation: MFA requires multiple verification factors (something you know, have, or are) for stronger security than passwords alone.

questions.question: Which malware type encrypts user files and demands payment for decryption?
questions.explanation: Ransomware encrypts victim's files and demands a ransom payment (usually cryptocurrency) for the decryption key.

questions.question: What does a firewall primarily do?
questions.explanation: Firewalls monitor and filter incoming/outgoing network traffic based on security rules, blocking unauthorized access.

questions.question: A user reports that an application crashes on startup. What is the first troubleshooting step?
options: B. Restart the application and check for error messages
questions.explanation: Always start with the simplest solution: restart the application, check error messages, and identify the specific issue before taking drastic measures.

questions.question: Which Windows tool can be used to check for corrupted system files?
questions.explanation: sfc /scannow (System File Checker) scans and repairs corrupted Windows system files. chkdsk checks disk integrity.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Change Management in IT?
options: B. Control and document changes to minimize risk
questions.explanation: Change Management processes ensure IT changes are planned, tested, approved, and documented to minimize service disruptions.

questions.question: Which Wi-Fi 6 feature allows a router to communicate with multiple devices simultaneously on both upload and download?
questions.explanation: Wi-Fi 6 supports 8x8 MU-MIMO (Multi-User Multiple-Input Multiple-Output), allowing the router to communicate with up to 8 devices simultaneously on both uplink and downlink streams.

questions.question: When configuring a Wi-Fi 6E access point, which frequency band requires new hardware compared to Wi-Fi 6?
questions.explanation: Wi-Fi 6E introduces the 6 GHz band (5.925-7.125 GHz), which requires Wi-Fi 6E-compatible hardware. Wi-Fi 6 devices cannot access the 6 GHz band.

questions.question: What is the maximum channel width available in Wi-Fi 6E?
questions.explanation: Wi-Fi 6E supports channel widths up to 160 MHz in the 6 GHz band, providing more available 160 MHz channels than the congested 5 GHz band.

questions.question: A technician needs to configure a SOHO router for maximum compatibility with both legacy and modern devices. Which wireless mode setting is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: Mixed mode allows the router to support legacy devices (802.11b/g/n) alongside modern Wi-Fi 5 (ac) and Wi-Fi 6 (ax) devices, ensuring maximum compatibility.

questions.question: Which security protocol should be selected when configuring a new Wi-Fi 6 network to ensure the highest level of security?
questions.explanation: WPA3-Personal with SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) provides the highest security for Wi-Fi 6 networks, protecting against offline dictionary attacks and offering forward secrecy.

questions.question: What does the Target Wake Time (TWT) feature in Wi-Fi 6 improve for connected devices?
options: B. Battery life and power efficiency
questions.explanation: Target Wake Time (TWT) schedules device wake/sleep cycles, significantly improving battery life for mobile and IoT devices by reducing time spent actively listening to the network.

questions.question: A user reports slow Wi-Fi speeds on a Wi-Fi 6 network in an apartment building with many neighboring networks. Which feature would most help improve performance?
questions.explanation: BSS Coloring helps Wi-Fi 6 devices distinguish between their own network (BSS) and neighboring networks, reducing contention and improving performance in dense environments like apartment buildings.

questions.question: Which Wi-Fi 6 feature divides a channel into smaller resource units to serve multiple devices simultaneously?
questions.explanation: OFDMA (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access) divides a Wi-Fi channel into smaller sub-channels (resource units), allowing multiple devices to communicate simultaneously rather than waiting for their turn.

questions.question: When configuring a dual-band Wi-Fi 6 router, which band should be recommended for high-bandwidth activities like 4K streaming?
options: A. 2.4 GHz for its longer range
B. 5 GHz for its higher speeds and less congestion
C. 900 MHz for its wall penetration
D. Both bands provide identical performance
questions.explanation: The 5 GHz band offers higher data rates and is less congested than 2.4 GHz, making it ideal for high-bandwidth activities like 4K streaming and online gaming.

questions.question: What is the maximum theoretical speed of Wi-Fi 6 using 8x8 MU-MIMO and 160 MHz channels?
questions.explanation: Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax) has a maximum theoretical speed of 9.6 Gbps when using 8x8 MU-MIMO, 160 MHz channel width, and 1024-QAM modulation.

questions.question: Which wireless troubleshooting step should be performed first when a user cannot connect to a Wi-Fi 6 network?
options: B. Verify the device supports the network's security protocol and frequency band
questions.explanation: The first troubleshooting step is to verify device compatibility — older devices may not support WPA3 or the 6 GHz band (for Wi-Fi 6E), which would prevent connection.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of OFDMA over older OFDM used in Wi-Fi 5 and earlier?
options: B. OFDMA serves multiple devices simultaneously; OFDM serves one at a time
C. OFDMA only works on 2.4 GHz
questions.explanation: OFDM (used in Wi-Fi 5 and earlier) allocates the entire channel to one device at a time. OFDMA (used in Wi-Fi 6) subdivides the channel, allowing simultaneous communication with multiple devices.

questions.question: What is the minimum TPM (Trusted Platform Module) version required for Windows 11 installation?
questions.explanation: Windows 11 requires TPM 2.0 (Trusted Platform Module) as a minimum security feature for installation, providing hardware-based security for encryption keys and credentials.

questions.question: Which Windows 11 feature provides hardware-enforced stack protection and memory integrity?
questions.explanation: Core Isolation with Memory Integrity is a Windows 11 security feature that uses virtualization-based security (VBS) to protect core processes from malware by isolating them in a virtualized environment.

questions.question: In Windows 11, which feature allows users to run Android apps natively?
questions.explanation: Windows Subsystem for Android (WSA) allows Windows 11 users to run Android apps natively on their PC through the Amazon Appstore integration. Note: Microsoft ended support for WSA in 2025, but it was a key Windows 11 feature.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Windows 11 Snap Layouts?
options: A. To encrypt files on the desktop
B. To quickly arrange application windows into organized layouts
questions.explanation: Snap Layouts in Windows 11 allow users to hover over the maximize button and quickly arrange application windows into predefined layouts (side-by-side, 2x2 grid, etc.) for improved multitasking.

questions.question: Which Windows 11 feature creates a visual separation between applications to help users focus?
options: B. Virtual Desktops with custom backgrounds
questions.explanation: Windows 11 offers multiple focus-enhancing features: Snap Groups remember snapped window arrangements, Virtual Desktops allow separate workspaces with custom backgrounds, and Task View provides an overview of all open windows.

questions.question: In Windows 11, which tool replaces the legacy Control Panel for most system settings?
questions.explanation: The Settings app in Windows 11 is the modern, streamlined replacement for the Control Panel, with Microsoft gradually migrating all system configuration options to this unified interface.

questions.question: Which Windows 11 security feature requires a processor that supports virtualization-based security (VBS)?
questions.explanation: HVCI (Hypervisor-Protected Code Integrity), also known as Memory Integrity, uses virtualization-based security (VBS) to prevent attackers from injecting malicious code into protected system processes.

questions.question: What is the minimum RAM requirement for installing Windows 11 on a 64-bit system?
questions.explanation: Windows 11 requires a minimum of 4 GB of RAM for 64-bit installation, along with a compatible 64-bit processor, 64 GB storage, TPM 2.0, and Secure Boot capable UEFI firmware.

questions.question: Which Windows 11 command can be used to check if the system meets hardware requirements for upgrade?
options: B. PC Health Check app / WhyNotWin11
questions.explanation: The PC Health Check app (Microsoft's official tool) or third-party alternatives like WhyNotWin11 can verify whether a system meets all Windows 11 hardware requirements including TPM, Secure Boot, and CPU compatibility.

questions.question: Which Windows 11 Start Menu change differentiates it from Windows 10?
options: A. The Start Menu is now centered by default with a simplified layout
B. Live Tiles are replaced with a static grid of icons and recommended files
questions.explanation: Windows 11 redesigned the Start Menu to be centered by default (with option to align left) and replaced Live Tiles with a simplified grid of pinned apps and a 'Recommended' section showing recent files.

questions.question: What is the function of Windows 11's Focus Assist feature?
options: A. To improve Wi-Fi signal strength
B. To suppress notifications during designated focus periods
questions.explanation: Focus Assist in Windows 11 (renamed to 'Do Not Disturb' in later updates) suppresses notifications during specified focus periods, helping users minimize distractions during work or presentations.

questions.question: Which Windows 11 feature allows users to quickly switch between multiple virtual desktops with different wallpapers and app layouts?
questions.explanation: Task View in Windows 11 (accessed via Win+Tab or the taskbar button) allows users to create and switch between multiple virtual desktops, each with different wallpapers, open apps, and window arrangements.

questions.question: Which Linux command displays the current network configuration including IP address, subnet mask, and MAC address for all interfaces?
questions.explanation: The 'ip addr' or 'ip a' command displays detailed network interface information including IP addresses, subnet masks, and MAC addresses. It replaces the deprecated 'ifconfig' command on modern Linux distributions.

questions.question: In modern Linux distributions, which command has replaced 'ifconfig' for managing network interfaces?
questions.explanation: The 'ip' command from the iproute2 package has replaced 'ifconfig' on modern Linux distributions. It provides more functionality for managing network interfaces, routing, and tunnels.

questions.question: Which Linux command displays active network connections, routing tables, and interface statistics?
options: C. netstat (or ss on modern systems)
questions.explanation: Both 'netstat' and 'ss' display active connections and network statistics. 'ss' is the modern replacement recommended on newer Linux distributions as it's faster and more efficient than netstat.

questions.question: Which command is used in Linux to check disk usage of files and directories in a human-readable format?
questions.explanation: 'du -h' (disk usage with human-readable output) displays the space used by files and directories in KB, MB, or GB. 'df -h' shows free disk space on filesystems, not individual directory usage.

questions.question: What does the Linux command 'systemctl status sshd' display?
options: B. The status of the SSH daemon service including whether it is active and enabled
questions.explanation: 'systemctl status sshd' displays the current status of the sshd service, including whether it is active (running), enabled (starts at boot), recent log entries, and process information. systemctl replaces the older service command.

questions.question: Which Linux command is used to change file permissions using numeric notation (e.g., 755)?
questions.explanation: The 'chmod' command changes file permissions. Numeric notation like 755 means: owner (7=rwx), group (5=r-x), others (5=r-x). chown changes ownership, chgrp changes group, and umask sets default permissions.

questions.question: In Linux, which command lists all running processes with detailed information including CPU and memory usage?
questions.explanation: Both 'ps aux' (snapshot of all processes) and 'top' (interactive, continuously updating process viewer) list running processes with CPU and memory usage. 'top' is interactive while 'ps' provides a static snapshot.

questions.question: Which Linux command searches for files by name throughout the filesystem starting from the root directory?
questions.explanation: 'find / -name [filename]' searches the entire filesystem starting from root for files matching the specified name. 'locate' uses a prebuilt database (faster but may be outdated). 'whereis' finds binary, source, and man files.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'journalctl' command in modern Linux systems using systemd?
options: B. To query and view system logs managed by systemd-journald
questions.explanation: 'journalctl' queries and displays system logs collected by systemd-journald. It has largely replaced reading individual log files in /var/log on systemd-based distributions.

questions.question: Which Linux command would a technician use to safely shut down the system in 5 minutes with a warning message to users?
questions.explanation: 'shutdown -h +5 [message]' schedules a halt (shutdown) in 5 minutes with an optional warning message broadcast to all logged-in users. -r would reboot instead of shutting down.

questions.question: Which command shows the routing table on a modern Linux system?
questions.explanation: Both 'ip route' (modern, preferred) and 'netstat -r' (older but still works) display the routing table. The 'route' command is considered deprecated but may still be available.

questions.question: In Linux, which command is used to add a new user to the system with a home directory?
questions.explanation: 'adduser' (interactive, user-friendly) or 'useradd -m' (creates home directory with the -m flag) adds a new user to the system. 'passwd' sets passwords, 'usermod' modifies users, and 'groupadd' adds groups.

questions.question: Which interface provides the fastest data transfer speeds for consumer SSDs, with support for up to 7,000+ MB/s sequential read?
options: B. NVMe Gen4 over PCIe x4
questions.explanation: NVMe Gen4 (PCIe 4.0 x4) supports speeds up to ~7,000-7,500 MB/s sequential read, far exceeding SATA III's maximum of ~600 MB/s. NVMe Gen5 can reach even higher speeds (up to ~12,000-14,000 MB/s).

questions.question: What is the physical form factor commonly used for NVMe SSDs in modern laptops and desktops?
questions.explanation: M.2 2280 (22mm wide, 80mm long) is the most common form factor for NVMe SSDs. The 'M.2' slot connects via PCIe lanes, and '2280' denotes the dimensions in millimeters.

questions.question: What is the key difference between SATA SSDs and NVMe SSDs in terms of connectivity?
options: A. SATA SSDs use the PCIe bus; NVMe SSDs use the SATA bus
B. SATA SSDs use the AHCI protocol over SATA; NVMe SSDs use the NVMe protocol over PCIe
C. SATA SSDs are always faster than NVMe SSDs
D. NVMe SSDs require external power adapters
questions.explanation: SATA SSDs use the AHCI protocol over the SATA interface (max ~600 MB/s), while NVMe SSDs use the NVMe protocol over PCIe lanes (up to 7,000+ MB/s Gen4), providing significantly higher throughput.

questions.question: Which NVMe SSD specification provides hardware-level encryption without impacting performance?
questions.explanation: OPAL 2.0 and TCG Pyrite are self-encrypting drive (SED) specifications that provide hardware-level encryption on NVMe SSDs without CPU overhead, protecting data at rest.

questions.question: What does the 'QLC' in QLC NAND refer to, and how does it compare to TLC NAND?
options: A. Quad-Level Cell stores 4 bits per cell vs 3 bits in TLC
B. Quick-Link Cache provides faster speeds than TLC
C. Quality-Level Certified means higher reliability than TLC
D. Quad-Layer Chip has more physical layers than TLC
questions.explanation: QLC (Quad-Level Cell) NAND stores 4 bits per memory cell compared to TLC's 3 bits, offering higher density and lower cost per GB at the expense of endurance and write performance.

questions.question: Which type of NAND flash memory offers the best balance of performance, endurance, and cost for consumer NVMe SSDs?
questions.explanation: TLC (Triple-Level Cell) NAND offers the best balance for consumer SSDs — good performance, reasonable endurance, and affordable pricing. SLC is fastest but expensive; QLC is cheapest but slower with lower endurance.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a DRAM cache on an NVMe SSD?
options: B. To store the mapping table for flash translation layer (FTL), improving random access performance
questions.explanation: The DRAM cache on an NVMe SSD stores the flash translation layer (FTL) mapping table, which tracks where logical data is physically stored on NAND, significantly improving random read/write performance.

questions.question: What is 'wear leveling' in SSD technology?
options: A. A method to physically clean the SSD
B. A technique that evenly distributes write operations across all NAND cells to maximize lifespan
C. A way to measure SSD temperature
D. A process to format the drive
questions.explanation: Wear leveling is an SSD controller technique that distributes write operations evenly across all NAND flash cells, preventing premature wear on frequently written blocks and extending the drive's lifespan.

questions.question: Which technology allows an NVMe SSD to directly access system RAM to speed up data transfers?
questions.explanation: RDMA (Remote Direct Memory Access) allows NVMe-oF (NVMe over Fabrics) devices to directly access system memory without CPU involvement, significantly reducing latency in data center environments.

questions.question: Which RAID level provides both striping and parity, requiring a minimum of three drives and allowing recovery from a single drive failure?
questions.explanation: RAID 5 uses block-level striping with distributed parity across all drives. It requires a minimum of 3 drives and can withstand the failure of any single drive without data loss.

questions.question: What does the NVMe 'TRIM' command (or 'deallocate') do?
options: A. Physically trims the SSD circuit board
B. Informs the SSD which blocks are no longer in use, improving write performance and longevity
questions.explanation: TRIM (or deallocate in NVMe) informs the SSD controller which data blocks are no longer in use by the file system, allowing the controller to proactively erase them and improve both write performance and SSD longevity.

questions.question: Which M.2 keying notch configuration supports both SATA and PCIe/NVMe SSDs?
questions.explanation: B+M keying (notches on both sides) indicates a socket that supports both SATA and PCIe/NVMe M.2 SSDs. M key supports PCIe/NVMe only, while B key typically supports SATA or PCIe x2.

questions.question: Which authentication method provides the highest level of security by requiring something the user knows, has, and is?
questions.explanation: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) requires two or more verification factors: something you know (password), something you have (token/phone), and/or something you are (biometric). It provides stronger security than 2FA alone.

questions.question: What is the primary security risk of using the same password across multiple websites?
options: B. If one site is breached, all accounts using that password are compromised
C. It violates password length requirements
questions.explanation: Password reuse is dangerous because a breach on one site exposes credentials for all other sites using the same password. Attackers use 'credential stuffing' to automatically try stolen username/password pairs across multiple sites.

questions.question: Which type of malware encrypts user files and demands payment for the decryption key?
questions.explanation: Ransomware encrypts victim's files and demands payment (ransom) in exchange for the decryption key. Modern ransomware variants also exfiltrate data for double-extortion tactics.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a BIOS/UEFI password?
options: A. To encrypt all files on the hard drive
B. To prevent unauthorized access to system firmware settings and boot order changes
C. To speed up the boot process
D. To connect to Wi-Fi networks
questions.explanation: A BIOS/UEFI password restricts access to firmware settings, preventing unauthorized users from changing boot order, disabling security features, or altering hardware configurations.

questions.question: Which security practice ensures that data cannot be recovered from a storage device before disposal?
options: B. Data sanitization / Secure erase (NIST SP 800-88)
C. Deleting files to Recycle Bin
questions.explanation: Data sanitization methods like secure erase, degaussing, or physical destruction (per NIST SP 800-88 guidelines) ensure data is irrecoverable. Simple formatting or deletion does not erase data.

questions.question: What is the difference between a virus and a worm?
options: A. Viruses require user action to spread; worms self-replicate across networks
B. Worms only infect Apple computers
C. Viruses cannot be detected by antivirus software
questions.explanation: A virus requires user action (opening a file, running a program) to spread, while a worm is a self-replicating malware that spreads automatically across networks without user intervention.

questions.question: Which email security technique verifies that an email message came from the domain it claims to come from?
questions.explanation: SPF (Sender Policy Framework), DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail), and DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication) work together to verify email sender authenticity and prevent spoofing and phishing.

questions.question: What is the purpose of disabling unused ports and services on a system?
options: B. To reduce the attack surface available to potential attackers
questions.explanation: Disabling unused ports and services is a hardening technique that reduces the attack surface — the fewer entry points available, the fewer opportunities attackers have to exploit vulnerabilities.

questions.question: Which social engineering technique involves creating a fabricated scenario to trick someone into providing information or access?
questions.explanation: Pretexting involves creating a believable false scenario (pretext) to manipulate victims into divulging information or granting access. The attacker impersonates someone with a legitimate need for the information.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of keeping operating systems and applications updated with the latest patches?
options: A. To add new features only
B. To fix security vulnerabilities and protect against known exploits
C. To slow down the computer
D. To change the user interface
questions.explanation: Software updates and patches primarily address discovered security vulnerabilities, bug fixes, and stability improvements. Unpatched systems are vulnerable to known exploits actively used by attackers.

questions.question: Which physical security control uses electromagnetic fields to detect unauthorized items passing through an entrance?
options: B. EAS (Electronic Article Surveillance) / Portal scanner
questions.explanation: EAS (Electronic Article Surveillance) systems use electromagnetic or RFID technology at entry/exit points to detect tagged items being removed without authorization, commonly used in retail and data center environments.

questions.question: What is the best practice for securing a mobile device that contains sensitive company data?
options: A. Only use it on public Wi-Fi
B. Enable remote wipe, encryption, screen lock, and MDM enrollment
C. Share the unlock password with coworkers
D. Disable all security features for convenience
questions.explanation: Best practices for securing mobile devices include: enabling encryption, strong screen locks, remote wipe capability, and MDM (Mobile Device Management) enrollment for centralized policy enforcement.

questions.question: Which cloud service model provides virtual machines, storage, and networking resources that users can configure and manage?
questions.explanation: IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) provides virtualized computing resources — VMs, storage, and networking — that users configure and manage. Examples: AWS EC2, Azure VMs, Google Compute Engine.

questions.question: A company wants to use Microsoft 365 for email and productivity without managing servers. Which cloud model is this?
questions.explanation: Microsoft 365 is SaaS (Software as a Service) — the provider manages everything (applications, data, runtime, servers), and users simply access the software via the internet.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of a hybrid cloud deployment?
options: A. All data is stored exclusively on-premises
B. Combines the security of private cloud with the scalability of public cloud
questions.explanation: Hybrid cloud combines on-premises/private cloud infrastructure with public cloud resources, allowing organizations to keep sensitive data on-premises while leveraging public cloud scalability for less sensitive workloads.

questions.question: In client-side virtualization, what is the role of the hypervisor?
options: B. To create and manage virtual machines by abstracting hardware resources
questions.explanation: A hypervisor (Type 1 or Type 2) creates and manages virtual machines by abstracting and allocating physical hardware resources (CPU, memory, storage) to each VM.

questions.question: What is the key difference between a Type 1 and Type 2 hypervisor?
options: A. Type 1 runs on bare metal; Type 2 runs on top of a host operating system
B. Type 1 is slower than Type 2
C. Type 2 does not support virtual machines
D. Type 1 requires a host OS
questions.explanation: Type 1 (bare metal) hypervisors run directly on hardware without a host OS (VMware ESXi, Hyper-V). Type 2 (hosted) hypervisors run as applications on a host OS (VMware Workstation, VirtualBox).

questions.question: Which cloud characteristic allows resources to be automatically provisioned and deprovisioned based on demand?
questions.explanation: Rapid elasticity is the cloud characteristic that enables resources to be automatically scaled up or down based on workload demand, often appearing unlimited to the consumer.

questions.question: What is a 'snapshot' in virtualization?
options: A. A physical photograph of the server
B. A point-in-time capture of a VM's state that can be reverted to
C. A backup of only the virtual network
questions.explanation: A VM snapshot captures the state, data, and configuration of a virtual machine at a specific point in time, allowing administrators to revert to that state if needed for recovery or testing.

questions.question: Which cloud deployment model is dedicated to a single organization and can be hosted on-premises or by a third party?
questions.explanation: A private cloud is dedicated to a single organization, providing exclusive use of cloud infrastructure. It can be managed on-premises or by a third-party provider, offering maximum control and security.

questions.question: What does the cloud characteristic 'measured service' mean?
options: A. All cloud resources are free
B. Resource usage is monitored, controlled, and reported for billing optimization
C. Network speed is always measured at 1 Gbps
D. Only storage resources are tracked
questions.explanation: Measured service means cloud systems automatically control, monitor, and report resource usage (storage, processing, bandwidth), providing transparency for both the provider and consumer for billing and optimization.

questions.question: A developer needs a platform to build, test, and deploy applications without managing underlying servers or storage. Which cloud model best fits?
questions.explanation: PaaS (Platform as a Service) provides a managed development platform with tools, runtime, and middleware, allowing developers to focus on coding without managing underlying infrastructure. Examples: Heroku, Google App Engine, Azure App Service.
